extra.message: User-controlled pageId parameter is concatenated directly into SQL query string via string interpolation ('+pageId) without parameterization. Attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the /dashboard/<pageId> route (controllers/Dashboard.py:8).

Attacker-controlled data from request.form['data_obj'] (hex-decoded on line 23) is written to pickle.hacker and deserialized via pickle.load(). This allows arbitrary remote code execution because pickle deserialization can execute arbitrary Python code during object reconstruction. Exploitable via POST to /about/sync.

yaml.load() is called without a SafeLoader on base64-decoded user input from the URL path /information/<input>. PyYAML's yaml.load() can deserialize arbitrary Python objects enabling remote code execution. User input flows directly from URL parameter (line 13) to base64 decode (line 17) to yaml.load() (line 18).

request.url is inserted into an HTML template string via .format() (line 94), then the result is rendered by render_template_string() (line 95). This enables both reflected XSS (raw HTML/JS via URL) and server-side template injection (Jinja2 expressions like {{config}} in the URL are evaluated server-side), potentially leaking secrets like SECRET_KEY.

User-controlled 'search' form input is rendered with {% autoescape false %} in the template, bypassing Jinja2's HTML escaping. Combined with the X-XSS-Protection header set to 0 in controllers/Search.py:11, this enables reflected XSS via POST /search.

User-controlled 'link' field (item[3]) from the messages table is rendered directly in an href attribute. Jinja2 HTML escaping does not filter URL protocols, so an attacker can inject a javascript: URL (e.g., javascript:alert(1)) via POST /messaging/new, achieving stored XSS when other users click the link.

Deserialized pickle content is rendered in the template with {% autoescape false %}, disabling Jinja2's HTML escaping. An attacker exploiting the pickle RCE on /about/sync can embed malicious HTML/JavaScript in the deserialized object for stored XSS.

The /confidential endpoint's only authorization check is if(session['loggedin']) on line 8, which merely verifies the key exists in the session. Any visitor who hits /webmaster first (Webmaster.py:12 sets session['loggedin'] for all users) can access this protected page without valid credentials.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The /webmaster endpoint unconditionally sets session['loggedin'] for every visitor without any authentication check. This directly enables bypassing the authorization check in Confidential.py, allowing unauthorized access to the /confidential endpoint.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Hardcoded credentials check: if(password == 'admin' and username == 'admin'). The username and password 'admin'/'admin' are hardcoded in the application source code, allowing anyone who knows them to authenticate.

The XML_download() function takes the 'example_file' value directly from user POST form input and passes it to open() (line 30) and send_file() (line 31) without any path validation or sanitization. An attacker can POST to /validator/getXML with example_file=../../etc/passwd to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory (Path Traversal)

extra.message: User-controlled filename (from uploaded file) is concatenated directly into the save path: 'uploads/'+filename. The allowed_file() check only validates the extension (e.g., txt, pdf, png) but does not strip path traversal sequences. A filename like '../../app.py' can be used if the target file has an approved extension, enabling arbitrary file overwrite outside the uploads directory.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory (Path Traversal)

extra.message: parseString() from xml.dom.pulldom is called on user-supplied XML from request.form['customers'] with default (unsafe) parser settings. The default Python XML parser processes external entities, allowing XXE attacks such as reading local files, SSRF, and denial of service via entity expansion.

cwe: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

extra.message: A system_call() function is registered as a Flask context processor (available in all templates) that executes arbitrary shell commands via os.popen(command).read() with no sanitization, filtering, or restriction. While current templates use hardcoded commands, this utility exposes command injection as a global template helper available to any template.

cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command (OS Command Injection)

extra.message: Flask application runs with app.debug = True, enabling the Werkzeug debugger. This exposes interactive Python stack traces with a console on errors, potentially allowing remote code execution and information disclosure including source code, environment variables, and the SECRET_KEY.

Flask SECRET_KEY is set to the hardcoded string 'random', which is trivially guessable. Combined with debug mode, weak password hashes in the DB, and predictable UUID session tokens, this allows session forgery and cookie tampering by any attacker.

The X-XSS-Protection response header is explicitly set to '0', disabling the browser's built-in reflected XSS filter. Combined with the reflected XSS in the search template (autoescape=false), this removes a defense-in-depth layer against XSS attacks.

CORS is configured with origins='*' (wildcard), allowing any external domain to make cross-origin requests to this application. Combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials being set in Confidential.py:10 (which explicitly allows cookie/session transmission cross-origin), this enables credential-theft and CSRF attacks from any malicious website.

cwe: CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
